channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.description:

item.title: The Opportunity rover's famous last words — "my battery is low and it's getting dark" — were never actually sent from Mars. The real story of where that sentence came from is stranger, and somehow sadder - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Opportunity rover's famous last words — "my battery is low and it's getting dark" — were never actually sent from Mars. The real story of where that sentence came from is stranger, and somehow sadder  Space Daily
  2. The Opportunity rover survived on Mars for 14 years — roughly 55 times longer than its 90-day design mission — before a 2018 global dust storm so vast it turned "day into night" across the entire planet buried its solar panels, and despite NASA sending more t  Space Daily

item.title: A space telescope orbiting Earth just picked up the same strange signature buried in every kind of cosmic ray it can detect — and it's a fingerprint physicists have been quietly waiting on since 1912 - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A space telescope orbiting Earth just picked up the same strange signature buried in every kind of cosmic ray it can detect — and it's a fingerprint physicists have been quietly waiting on since 1912  Space Daily
  2. Ultrahigh-energy Cosmic Rays May Be Ultraheavy in Origin  Universe Today
  3. The Universe’s Most Powerful Particles May Be Even Stranger Than Scientists Thought  SciTechDaily
  4. Cosmic rays change behavior in a way that could reveal where they really come from  Earth.com

item.title: The mystery of T. rex’s tiny arms might finally be solved - CNN

  4. Why were T. rex’s arms so tiny? Paleontologists finally find an answer.  Popular Science

item.title: Voyager 1’s famous Pale Blue Dot photograph was nearly never taken — Carl Sagan pushed NASA to turn the camera back toward Earth after the planetary mission was over, while engineers worried the Sun’s glare could damage the optics - Space Daily
item.description: Voyager 1’s famous Pale Blue Dot photograph was nearly never taken — Carl Sagan pushed NASA to turn the camera back toward Earth after the planetary mission was over, while engineers worried the Sun’s glare could damage the optics  Space Daily

item.title: When a Soviet rover went silent on the Moon in 1971, scientists assumed it was gone for good — but nearly forty years later, the reflector strapped to its back answered a laser pulse from Earth as if no time had passed at all - Space Daily
item.description:
1. When a Soviet rover went silent on the Moon in 1971, scientists assumed it was gone for good — but nearly forty years later, the reflector strapped to its back answered a laser pulse from Earth as if no time had passed at all  Space Daily
  2. This Soviet rover was thought lost on the moon, then it started sending signals again nearly 40 years later  MSN
  3. The Soviet Lunokhod 1 rover went silent on the Moon in 1971, then scientists found its lost reflector in 2010 and got a signal bright enough to reopen a forgotten corner of lunar science  Space Daily

  2. Silk can be transformed into a super-material stronger than bone  Earth.com
  3. Silk fused into Kevlar-like material for implants and sensors  Institution of Mechanical Engineers - IMechE
  4. Silk made into strong plastic-like materials with 6G potential  Technology Org
  5. Fused silk under increasing levels of temperature and pressure [IMAGE]  EurekAlert!

item.title: NASA's Voyager 1 probe is about to reach a distance of 1 light-day from Earth. Here's why this cosmic milestone is so huge - BBC Sky at Night Magazine
item.description:
1. NASA's Voyager 1 probe is about to reach a distance of 1 light-day from Earth. Here's why this cosmic milestone is so huge  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  2. Voyager 1 is now so far from Earth that a signal traveling at the speed of light takes more than 22 hours to reach it — so when engineers send a command, they can wait nearly two days to know whether the spacecraft responded  Space Daily
  3. 6 mind-blowing facts as Voyager 1 nears ‘one light-day’ from Earth  WION
  4. Voyager 1's signal now takes more than 23 hours to reach Earth, and by the time NASA receives the next status check the spacecraft will already be 1.5 million kilometres further into interstellar space  Space Daily

item.title: Michael Collins, dubbed by the press "the loneliest man in history" while orbiting the far side of the Moon for roughly forty-seven minutes at a time, gently corrected the description — he said he felt isolated, but never lonely - Space Daily

item.title: The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they've found the scraps - KSL.com
item.description:
1. The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they've found the scraps  KSL.com
  2. Astronomers Spot Remains Of Galaxy Devoured By Milky Way  NDTV
  3. NASA Discovers Eerie Remains Of A Galaxy The Milky Way Devoured  HotHardware
  4. Study: Strange stars may actually be remnants of a galaxy the Milky Way 'ate'  WKRC
  5. Scientists think they may have found the remains of an entire galaxy that the Milky Way absorbed billions of years ago — 20 unusual stars hiding inside our own galactic disk, possibly the leftovers of a small galaxy our own home swallowed when it was still you  Space Daily

item.title: In 1995, NASA’s Galileo spacecraft sent a probe into Jupiter’s atmosphere that kept transmitting for just 58 minutes as it fell, returning the first direct readings from inside the giant planet before rising heat and pressure silenced it for good - Space Daily
item.description: In 1995, NASA’s Galileo spacecraft sent a probe into Jupiter’s atmosphere that kept transmitting for just 58 minutes as it fell, returning the first direct readings from inside the giant planet before rising heat and pressure silenced it for good  Space Daily

item.title: Carl Sagan's team considered sending a nude photograph of a man and a pregnant woman on the Voyager Golden Record, but after the controversy over the nude Pioneer plaque, the final record used a silhouette instead - Space Daily
item.description: Carl Sagan's team considered sending a nude photograph of a man and a pregnant woman on the Voyager Golden Record, but after the controversy over the nude Pioneer plaque, the final record used a silhouette instead  Space Daily

item.title: The Arecibo message — humanity’s most famous deliberate radio message to another civilization — was aimed at a star cluster about 25,000 light-years away in 1974, meaning even an immediate reply would not reach Earth until around 52,000 CE - Space Daily
item.description: The Arecibo message — humanity’s most famous deliberate radio message to another civilization — was aimed at a star cluster about 25,000 light-years away in 1974, meaning even an immediate reply would not reach Earth until around 52,000 CE  Space Daily

item.title: Consciousness might not be something the brain creates — it might be a fundamental feature of reality itself, more like gravity than like a thought — and one of the most credentialed neuroscientists alive is now arguing that mainstream science has been wrong - Space Daily

item.title: Oldest Wooden Tool May Be a 430,000-Year-Old "Stick" Found in Greece - ZME Science
item.description:
1. Oldest Wooden Tool May Be a 430,000-Year-Old "Stick" Found in Greece  ZME Science
  2. Scientists discover the oldest wooden tools ever used by humans  ScienceDaily
  3. A 430,000-year-old digging stick is the oldest wooden hand tool ever found  Martin Cid Magazine
  4. Ancient humans were using sophisticated wooden tools 4,30,000 years ago  India Today

item.title: In 1908 something exploded over a remote part of Siberia with the force of roughly 1,000 Hiroshima bombs, flattened 80 million trees, and was heard 600 miles away — and more than a century later, scientists still cannot fully agree on whether it was an asteroi - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 1908 something exploded over a remote part of Siberia with the force of roughly 1,000 Hiroshima bombs, flattened 80 million trees, and was heard 600 miles away — and more than a century later, scientists still cannot fully agree on whether it was an asteroi  Space Daily
  2. In 1908, something exploded in the sky over Siberia with hundreds of times the energy of the Hiroshima bomb, flattening more than 2,000 square kilometres of forest. When scientists finally reached the site years later, they found no crater at all  Space Daily

item.title: Every GPS satellite is launched with a clock deliberately set to run slow, because Einstein's relativity speeds it up by about 38 microseconds a day once in orbit — and without that built-in correction, your phone's location would drift by roughly ten kilometres a d - Space Daily
item.description: Every GPS satellite is launched with a clock deliberately set to run slow, because Einstein's relativity speeds it up by about 38 microseconds a day once in orbit — and without that built-in correction, your phone's location would drift by roughly ten kilometres a d  Space Daily

item.title: Human creativity may have been forged by hardship rather than abundance — according to a new study of 146,000-year-old stone tools found in central China and dated to one of the harshest Ice Ages of early human history - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Human creativity may have been forged by hardship rather than abundance — according to a new study of 146,000-year-old stone tools found in central China and dated to one of the harshest Ice Ages of early human history  Space Daily
  2. A Radical Innovation Helped Archaic Humans Survive a Harsh Ice Age  ScienceAlert
  3. Chinese scientists suggest harsh environments foster early human creativity  South China Morning Post
  4. 146,000-Year-Old Discovery Rewrites the Story of Human Creativity  SciTechDaily
  5. Crystals inside an ancient bone in central China just overturned a 146,000-year-old story about humans  The Times of India
